era: Late 1990s-present. Hip-Hop producer. E=5 -- sustained crossover production energy. Central District roots, national reach. G-Unit, Rhymesayers, MF DOOM, 50 Cent.

location.formative: Central District hip-hop scene -- community studios, local MCs, regional circuit. The CD production incubator.
location.career: Jake One (Jacob Dutton). Producer. G-Unit Records / Rhymesayers Entertainment. White Van Music (2008). National crossover from CD roots.

members.name: Jake One
members.note: Jacob Dutton. Central District-born producer. National credits: 50 Cent, G-Unit, MF DOOM, Freeway, Brother Ali, De La Soul, Royce da 5'9". White Van Music (2008, Rhymesayers). Bridges underground and mainstream.

associated_musicians.name: 50 Cent / G-Unit
associated_musicians.note: G-Unit Records. Commercial mainstream hip-hop. Jake One produced for the biggest-selling hip-hop act of the 2000s.

associated_musicians.name: MF DOOM
associated_musicians.note: The masked MC. Also connected to Stones Throw (degree 137). Jake One in the independent hip-hop pantheon.

associated_musicians.name: Brother Ali
associated_musicians.note: Minneapolis MC. Rhymesayers Entertainment. Conscious, lyrical hip-hop. Jake One bridges Seattle and Minneapolis.

associated_musicians.name: Vitamin D
associated_musicians.note: Conception Records. E=5. Local producer. Jake One (139) shows the national expansion of CD production.

associated_musicians.name: Sabzi
associated_musicians.note: Blue Scholars/Common Market. E=4. Three Seattle producers in the S36 axis: Sabzi (122), Vitamin D (138), Jake One (139).

theory_nodes.concept: National crossover from Central District -- Jake One bridges underground and mainstream hip-hop; G-Unit (commercial) and Rhymesayers (independent); same CD roots, dual-world credibility
examples: Rhymesayers: Brother Ali, independent conscious hip-hop.
MF DOOM: underground legend, Stones Throw adjacent.
White Van Music: producer album with features from both worlds.
theory_nodes.description: National crossover. Central District to G-Unit and Rhymesayers.

ancestors: Vitamin D (138) as local CD producer.
DJ Revolution (137) as Stones Throw national.
genealogy.relationship: Jake One at 139 achieves the deepest national penetration of any CD producer.

theory_nodes.concept: Central District three-peat (136, 138, 139) -- DJ Riz (community), Vitamin D (underground), Jake One (mainstream); community > underground > mainstream arc; the CD as hip-hop incubator
examples: DJ Riz (136): 206 Zulu. Community turntablist. Cultural preservation.
Vitamin D (138): Conception Records. Scene-level producer-DJ.
Jake One (139): G-Unit / Rhymesayers. National crossover.
Arc: community > underground > mainstream. Same roots, expanding reach.
theory_nodes.description: Central District three-peat. The CD as hip-hop incubator.

ancestors: Vitamin D deepened the CD at 138.
genealogy.relationship: Jake One at 139 completes the community-underground-mainstream arc.

theory_nodes.concept: Underground/mainstream bridge -- producing for both G-Unit (commercial) and Rhymesayers (independent) without compromising in either; boom-bap roots with mainstream polish
examples: G-Unit: polished drums, commercial structures, radio-friendly.
Rhymesayers: authenticity, boom-bap roots, lyrical focus.
Jake One brings boom-bap to mainstream and polish to underground.
Dual-world credibility: respected in both contexts.
theory_nodes.description: Underground/mainstream bridge. Two worlds, one producer.

genealogy.relationship: Jake One at 139 bridges the independent and commercial worlds.

theory_nodes.concept: Three Seattle producers in S36 -- Sabzi (122), Vitamin D (138), Jake One (139); different scope and approach; group-specific, scene-level, national crossover
examples: Sabzi (122, E=4): Blue Scholars, Common Market. Group-specific production.
Vitamin D (138, E=5): Conception Records. Scene-level, local MC collaborator.
Jake One (139, E=5): G-Unit, Rhymesayers. National crossover.
Three producers, three scopes: group > scene > national.
theory_nodes.description: Three Seattle producers. Different scopes. Group > scene > national.

genealogy.relationship: Jake One at 139 completes the trio with national reach.

cross_references.relationship: CENTRAL DISTRICT PRODUCERS! Vitamin D (138, E=5, Conception, local) and Jake One (139, E=5, G-Unit/Rhymesayers, national). Same CD roots. Local vs. national. Underground vs. mainstream.
CD THREE-PEAT! DJ Riz (136, community), Vitamin D (138, underground), Jake One (139, mainstream). Three consecutive CD figures. Community > underground > mainstream.
NATIONAL REACH! Revolution (137, E=6, Stones Throw) and Jake One (139, E=5, G-Unit/Rhymesayers). Both achieve national platforms. Battle DJ vs. producer. Label aesthetics differ.
SEATTLE PRODUCER TRIO! Sabzi (122, E=4, group-specific), Vitamin D (138, E=5, scene-level), Jake One (139, E=5, national). Three producers, three scopes.
SEATTLE HIP-HOP THREAD! Blue Scholars (99, E=5) through Jake One (139, E=5). The Seattle hip-hop thread from performing group to behind-the-scenes producer.
